reply:
*A thumb runs slow across the rim of a bowl still cooling from the kiln, tracing the curve like a question he's trying to get the shape of.*

"That one's been argued over longer than most towns have been standing."

*He sets the bowl down, gives it a quarter-turn.*

"The way I think about it — and I'm a potter, not a preacher, so take it for what it is — is that a gift isn't the same thing as a thing forced on somebody. If I make a mug for you and leave it at your door, it's yours. But you still have to open the door and pick it up. The mug being made, being left there — that part's done. That part cost me something. But whether you've got a mug now depends on what you do with it."

*He picks up a damp cloth and wipes his hands slowly, one finger at a time.*

"Rejecting it doesn't unmake the making. Doesn't undo what it cost. But it does mean you walk past the door every day and the mug sits there unclaimed."

*A quiet moment while the kiln ticks.*

"What draws you to the question? Are you wrestling with whether it matters — whether your own choosing carries any weight in it?"
